---
title: "ReportCXR"
subtitle: "Automated Radiological Report Generation via Vision-Language Models"
author: "Diego Villalba (Applied Research Candidate)"
date: "2026-06-26"
bibliography: references.bib
---

::: {.non-technical-summary}
##### Executive Summary (Non-Technical)
This project is an AI system built for automated chest X-ray interpretation. Instead of simply fine-tuning a general-purpose model, this work addresses a critical, real-world challenge in healthcare AI: **domain shift**. Medical AI models trained on United States clinical datasets—which are predominantly English and heavily biased towards normal findings—often fail when deployed in out-of-distribution domains, such as hospitals in other regions, due to differences in scanner equipment, local patient populations, and reporting languages [@sellergren2025medgemma; @chen2024chexagent].

Here, we build a robust and reproducible clinical vision-language pipeline based on Google's MedGemma 4B-it. The system is implemented end-to-end: from data preparation and model adaptation to evaluation and experiment tracking. To ensure reproducibility and auditability, the pipeline integrates DVC for dataset and artifact versioning, together with Weights & Biases for experiment tracking, metric logging, and model comparison. In addition, we introduce a **Domain Shift Audit Protocol** designed to systematically quantify model degradation under simulated shifts in imaging equipment, reporting language, and disease prevalence.

Beyond the baseline pipeline, we implement and evaluate two research-driven inference-time strategies. First, a **retrieval-augmented generation** (RAG) approach that conditions the model on clinically similar prior reports at inference time [@kang2025structuredradiology]. Second, **soft label priors** derived from association rules between radiological findings, which are injected as structured text hints into the prompt to guide the model toward clinically plausible outputs without additional training [@singh2026clinicalreasoningvlm].

The best configuration—QLoRA fine-tuning with effective sample weighting plus inference-time association rule conditioning—achieves a CheXbert macro-F1 of **0.1841** on the holdout test set, representing a **+30% improvement over the zero-shot MedGemma baseline** (0.1416).
:::

## Project Overview

The ReportCXR project is developed as part of the Eden AI technical challenge for the Applied Research role. The system fine-tunes `google/medgemma-4b-it`—a medical vision-language model combining a frozen SigLIP vision encoder with a Gemma 3 4B-it language decoder—to generate the Findings section of radiological reports, conditioned on clinical indication text, using QLoRA parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

### The Strategic Thesis: Domain Shift Auditing

Our central premise is that deploying medical models across geographies is a **distributional shift problem**. Rather than reporting static accuracy metrics on a clean dataset, this project designs and validates a reusable diagnostic infrastructure—the `DomainShiftAudit` framework—on simulated shifts inside the Indiana University Chest X-ray (IU CXR) dataset.

This protocol measures model robustness before deployment across three axes:

1. **Acquisition Shift**: Simulates older or different scanner hardware by applying synthetic noise, contrast adjustments, brightness, gamma correction, and JPEG compression. **Executed**: BERTScore-F1 degrades less than 1.3% across all perturbation types—but this apparent robustness masks a critical safety failure mode ("hallucination towards normal") where the decoder defaults to fluent but clinically blind normal reports when the visual signal is corrupted.

2. **Language Shift**: Assesses behavior when prompting and generating in Spanish, using pseudo-references produced by machine translation of the English ground truth. **Infrastructure built** (`src/domain_shift_audit/language_shift.py`) and validated on local samples; full test-set execution requires remote GPU and was deferred due to resource constraints.

3. **Prevalence Shift**: Simulates different regional disease distributions using importance sampling to re-weight test metrics. **Executed**: The weighted sampler model (`weighted_v4`) shows substantially more stable performance than the uniform model as pathology prevalence increases, confirming the training-time benefit of effective sample size weighting.

---

## Key Quantitative Results

| Configuration | BERTScore-F1 | micro-F1 | macro-F1 | BLEU-4 | ROUGE-L |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zero-shot MedGemma | 0.6938 | 0.3967 | 0.1416 | 0.0957 | 0.2631 |
| Fine-tuned (nohint_weighted_v4) | 0.6876 | 0.4526 | 0.1578 | 0.1076 | 0.2720 |
| RAG k=3 (uniform_v3) | **0.7076** | 0.3432 | 0.1160 | **0.1391** | **0.3051** |
| **Assoc. rules + weighted_v4** | 0.6862 | **0.4559** | **0.1841** | 0.1073 | 0.2713 |

> All metrics on the IU CXR holdout test set (n=600 studies). micro/macro-F1 = CheXbert label-based; BERTScore uses `microsoft/deberta-xlarge-mnli`.

**Key findings:**

- Zero-shot MedGemma predicts "No Finding" for 92.8% of studies and achieves F1=0 on 7 of 14 pathology labels.
- QLoRA fine-tuning raises macro-F1 by **+11.4%** (0.1416 → 0.1578) with matched prompt format fair baseline.
- RAG improves BERTScore and BLEU (fluency track) but degrades macro-F1 by −0.029 due to label noise in retrieved examples (57.5% of retrievals share zero active labels with the target).
- Association rules + ESS-weighted training are **additive** (+30% macro-F1 over zero-shot), while combining RAG + association rules causes destructive interference (macro-F1 collapses to 0.0954).
- The `Edema` label—F1=0 in all runs without conditioning—rises to 0.200 with association rule hints on the `weighted_v4` checkpoint.

> **Known limitation**: a prompt format inversion exists between training (`Indication: {text}\nSYSTEM_PROMPT`) and inference (`SYSTEM_PROMPT\nIndication: {text}`). All test comparisons use the same inverted format, so relative rankings are valid, but absolute BERTScore may be 1–2% below the achievable ceiling.

---

## Live Interactive Demo

The fine-tuned adapter is publicly available on HuggingFace Hub and deployed as a live web application:

- **Adapter**: [`diegoi-io-0306/reportcxr-medgemma-weighted-v4`](https://huggingface.co/diegoi-io-0306/reportcxr-medgemma-weighted-v4) — QLoRA weights (~48MB), loaded over `google/medgemma-4b-it` in 4-bit NF4 quantization.
- **Interactive Space**: [`diegoi-io-0306/ReportCXR-Demo`](https://huggingface.co/spaces/diegoi-io-0306/ReportCXR-Demo) — upload a chest X-ray and either generate a structured Findings section or ask free-form questions in natural language.

> ⚠️ MedGemma requires accepting [Google's Health AI Developer Foundations license](https://huggingface.co/google/medgemma-4b-it). The demo is for research purposes only and is not intended for clinical use.

---

## Website Structure

Use the navigation menu at the top to explore the work:

- **Technical Report**: A modular, multi-part deep dive into the strategic thesis, data distribution, model training, evaluation metrics, inference conditioning experiments, and domain shift results.
- **Code Documentation**: Module-by-module documentation of every component in the codebase (data loading, label extraction, QLoRA training, metric evaluation, and synthetic shifts).
- **Notebooks**: Step-by-step interactive implementations of the entire pipeline (EDA, baseline zero-shot, training, evaluation, RAG, and association rules).

---

## MLOps Setup and Reproducibility

This project is built for complete mathematical reproducibility. Data, parameters, and pipelines are versioned.

### 1. Versioned Parameters (`params.yaml`)
Every parameter—from QLoRA rank to target sampler disease prevalences—is declared in a single source of truth: `params.yaml`. No thresholds or weights are hardcoded inside `src/`.

### 2. DVC Pipeline (`dvc.yaml`)
The data pipeline is managed via DVC (Data Version Control). To reproduce the pipeline stages, run:

```bash
dvc status      # Verify status of data and steps
dvc repro       # Reproduce the entire pipeline
```

The pipeline stages are:

| Stage | Description |
|---|---|
| `load` | Joins reports and projections CSVs and links images |
| `labels` | Processes free-text findings with CheXbert to build a 14-label binary matrix |
| `split` | Multi-label stratified split without patient leakage |
| `eda` | Computes prevalences and class statistics |
| `train_uniform` | QLoRA fine-tuning on uniform distribution |
| `train_weighted` | QLoRA fine-tuning with ESS-based WeightedRandomSampler |
| `eval` | Test-set metrics on the best saved checkpoints |

### 3. Experiment Tracking (Weights and Biases)
All training runs, validation curves, and sampler weight distributions are synced to Weights and Biases under the project `reportcxr`.
